          what, they violate the law when they tend to destroy, through public revelation . . .

honor [considération] justly or unjustly acquired . . . . 110

Similar accounts of the right to privacy as a right to “honor” made their way into other

textbook accounts by the end of the nineteenth century as well, 111 and the importance of

the protection of “honorable” privacy was something close to orthodoxy in France by the

1890s.

V

Similar developments were underway, by the late nineteenth century, in Germany.

There were important differences, though. The world of the developing German law of

privacy was not the overheated world of Paris high art, with its nude models and

lascivious photos. Instead it was the heady world of the German philosophy of free will.

Moreover, German privacy law developed more slowly than French law, not really

establishing itself before the middle of the twentieth century. And when German privacy

law did establish itself, it was in connection with the painful experience of Nazism.

The German tradition of “privacy” protections is perhaps easiest to understand if

we emphasize one point: German privacy law grew in large part out of an effort to create

a richer German alternative to western ideas of liberty. The protection of “privacy,” in

the German tradition, is regarded as an aspect of the protection of one of the most

baffling of German juristic creations: “personality.” “Personality” is a characteristically

110
    Beaussire, Principes du Droit, 377-378.
          [L]’honneur privée, quelle que soit la valeur en lui-même et dans son origine, doit être à l’abri de
          toute atteinte. On a pu railler ce qu’il y’a d’excessif dans la maxime que “la vie privée doit être
          murée” . . . [Nevertheless], les curiosités et les médisances du monde peuvent être . . . plus ou
          moins innocentes ou plus ou moin blâmables : elles violent le droit quand elles tendent à détruire
          par une révélvation publique . . . une considération justement ou même injustement acquise . . .
For his identification of “honneur” and “considération,” see id. 377.
111
    E.g. A. Boistel, Cours de Philosophie de Droit (Paris: Fontemoing, 1899), 1: 243-247.

38
 dense concept, with roots in the philosophies of Kant, Humboldt and Hegel. As any

account of German law will tell you, the German law of “personality” is a law of

freedom—the law of the “Inner Space, in which [humans] develop freely and self-

responsibly their personalities.”112 Any account will also tell you that this law of

freedom has especially flourished since the 1950s, when Germans applied the lessons

they had learned from the Nazi disaster. 113 Any standard account will tell you these

things—but if you are the average American lawyer, you can be forgiven for not finding

them easy to grasp. What is the “Inner Space”? How does guaranteeing the freedom of

the “Inner Space” represent an antidote to Nazism?

To get a firmer handle on these difficult, and occasionally comical, concepts, we
